coredata.dc:title: Fungus-derived photoluminescent carbon nanodots for ultrasensitive detection of Hg2+ ions and photoinduced bactericidal activity
coredata.dc:creator: Venkateswarlu, Sada
Viswanath, Buddolla
Reddy, Ankireddy Seshadri
Yoon, Minyoung
coredata.dc:description: Herein, we present a sustainable solvent-free synthetic procedure to produce carbon nanodots from common edible mushrooms (Pleurotus spp.). The resulting mushroom carbon nanodots (MCDs) exhibit stable blue fluorescence with high quantum yield (25%). The MCDs are highly dispersible in water because of the enormous number of oxygen- and nitrogen-containing functional groups on the surface. The MCDs can be used as an effective fluorescent probe for label-free detection of Hg2+ ions (detection limit: 4.13nM). To improve the sensitivity, dihydrolipoic acid was attached to the surface of MCDs, resulting in ultra-sensitivity in Hg2+ ion sensing, with a detection limit as low as 17.4 pM. In addition, the MCDs can be used for the labeling of bacteria and as a photoinduced bactericidal agent. Light irradiation of E. coli treated with MCDs showed excellent bactericidal activity relative to the control. These sustainable and affordable carbon materials are potentially compatible for monitoring toxic metals and as a potent visible-light-responsive bactericidal probe.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Mushroom
Carbon dot
Fluorescence sensor
Hg2+ ion detection
Bactericidal effect

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: In vitro reactive oxygen species (ROS) assay
Synthesis and characterization of MCDs and DHLA@MCDs
The response of MCDs towards pH
Sensitivity and selectivity of MCDs towards Hg2+
The quenching mechanism of MCDs and DHLA@MCDs for Hg2+
The selectivity of MCDs and DHLA@MCDs towards other metal ions
Fluorescence imaging and photoinduced bactericidal study

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by a National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) grant funded by the Ministry of Education ( NRF-2014R1A1A2A16049980 , NRF-2016M2A2A6024480 , and NRF-2016R1D1A1B03930948 ). E. coli Nissle 1917 donated by C. S. Kathera, Sri Venkateswara University, Tirupati, India.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of synthesis of MCDs by thermal treatment at various temperatures and surface functionalization with DHLA.
(A-B) TEM and (C) HRTEM images of MCDs and (D) SAED pattern of MCDs. The inset in (B) shows the statistical size analysis.
FT-IR spectra of (i) pure MCDs, (ii) DHLA@MCDs and (iii) DHLA@MCDs treated with Hg2+ ions.

(A) XPS survey spectrum of (i) MCDs, (ii) DHLA@MCDs and (iii) DHLA@MCDs after treatment with Hg2+ ions, (B–E) high-resolution XPS spectra of C 1s, O 1s, N 1s and S 2p of (i) MCDs, (ii) DHLA@MCDs and (iii) DHLA@MCDs after treatment with Hg2+ ions, (F) Hg 4f HRXPS spectrum.
Illustration of temperature dependent nucleation of MCDs, TEM images at A) 100°C, B) 140°C and C) 170°C.

(A) (i) UV–vis absorption spectrum of bare MCDs, (ii) PL excitation spectra, (iii) emission spectra of MCDs. (Right inset) color of MCD solution without or with UV irradiation (365nm), (B) PL emission spectra of MCD solutions depending on excitation light wavelength.

Fluorescence emission spectra of the DHLA@MCDs in aqueous solution upon the addition of Hg2+ (0–100nM); (B) linear fit of quenched fluorescence intensity as a function of Hg2+ concentration; (C) Selectivity of DHLA@MCDs towards Hg2+ ions over various other metal ions (0.1M PBS, pH 7.0); D) Photographs of the DHLA@MCDs aqueous solutions without (top) and with (bottom) UV light (365nm).
Proposed binding and sensing mechanism of the DHLA@MCDs for Hg2+ ions.
SEM images of E. coli: (A–C) light irradiation without MCDs, (D–F) MCDs treated under dark conditions, and (G–I) MCDs treated with visible light irradiation for 12h.
Comparison of different fluorescent probes performances for Hg2+ detection.

head.ce:title: Fungus-derived photoluminescent carbon nanodots for ultrasensitive detection of Hg2+ ions and photoinduced bactericidal activity

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Nanochemistry, Gachon University, Sungnam 13120, Republic of Korea

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Bionanotechnology, Gachon University, Sungnam 13120, Republic of Korea

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemical & Biological Engineering, Gachon University, Sungnam 13120, Republic of Korea

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein, we present a sustainable solvent-free synthetic procedure to produce carbon nanodots from common edible mushrooms (Pleurotus spp.). The resulting mushroom carbon nanodots (MCDs) exhibit stable blue fluorescence with high quantum yield (25%). The MCDs are highly dispersible in water because of the enormous number of oxygen- and nitrogen-containing functional groups on the surface. The MCDs can be used as an effective fluorescent probe for label-free detection of Hg2+ ions (detection limit: 4.13nM). To improve the sensitivity, dihydrolipoic acid was attached to the surface of MCDs, resulting in ultra-sensitivity in Hg2+ ion sensing, with a detection limit as low as 17.4 pM. In addition, the MCDs can be used for the labeling of bacteria and as a photoinduced bactericidal agent. Light irradiation of E. coli treated with MCDs showed excellent bactericidal activity relative to the control. These sustainable and affordable carbon materials are potentially compatible for monitoring toxic metals and as a potent visible-light-responsive bactericidal probe.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon materials, such as fullerenes, graphene, carbon nanotubes, nanodiamonds, coal, and aerogels have inspired fascination and extensive study due to their wide diversity of technical applications, such as adsorption, catalysis, energy storage, bio-engineering, electrochemistry, mass-energy transport and wastewater treatment [1,2]. Among the many carbon allotropes, quasi-spherical carbon nanoparticles with a diameter of several nanometers (2–10nm), termed carbon dots (CDs), contain a mixture of sp3 and sp2 carbon atoms in different ratios [3]. In recent years, CDs have continued to garner research attention because of their fascinating features, superior water dispersible, unique fluorescence, excellent biocompatibility, high stability and low toxicity relative to metal-based quantum dots [4,5]. These capabilities allow innumerable applications, such as photodynamic therapy, bioimaging, photocatalysis, invisible security ink and bio-metal sensing [6–10]. During the past decade, various synthetic approaches to produce CDs have been widely reported, including arc discharge [11], electrochemical synthesis [12], acid oxidation [13], combustion [14], microwave-assisted synthesis [15], hydro/solvothermal and pyrolysis methods [16]. However, most of these approaches possess shortcomings, such as the usage of expensive and hazardous solvents, complex procedures, and harsh reaction conditions, all of which restrict the large-scale production of the CDs for analytical purposes. Therefore, development of a simple, eco-friendly and economical approach toward carbon fluorescent materials is highly desired. Apart from the above methods, a number of examples were reported on the synthesis of CDs by green synthesis using vegetables, fruits, and seafood etc., [17–21]. Nevertheless, the existing approaches have several shortcomings such as the addition of solvent (acids, base, ethanol, including water) for synthesis, insufficient property for applications, and difficulty for heteroatoms (N, S) incorporation into CDs [22–24]. To overcome these issues, we first propose a simple strategy to synthesize CDs using a natural and abundantly available edible mushroom that contains nitrogen element and does not require solvent in synthetic process, which can be used for various applications including heavy metal detection (Hg2+) and photoinduced bactericidal activity.

Aquatic pollution by heavy metal ions is a big worsen to biodiversity and human. Among the various heavy metals that causes water pollution, Hg2+ is one commonly affecting the neurologic, minamata disease, gastrointestinal and renal organ systems in any form and even in minute quantities [25]. Therefore, developing an effective analytical method for the sensitive (∼pM concentration) and selective detection of Hg2+ is particularly important for effective toxicity monitoring. A plethora of traditional techniques such as inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry, cold-vapor atomic fluorescence spectroscopy, polarography, voltammetry, colorimetric assays, and Auger electron spectroscopy have been employed, to analyze such heavy metal ions [26–28]. Nevertheless, these methods usually possess certain shortcomings, such as the usage of hazardous solvents, very expensive, tedious in operation, and the generation of waste products. Lately, fluorescent probes have drawn a great attention towards sensitive and selective detection of Hg2+ ions [29]. Various fluorescent probes such as metallic quantum dots (QD), organic dyes, fluorescent metal nanoparticles, organic polymers, and carbon dots (CD) are reported [30–33]. These materials also suffer from several drawbacks, like easy oxidation, high cost, toxicity, photobleaching and low quantum yield, which restrict their wide application. In this context, we have prepared high quantum yield and rapid dispersion of CDs for ultrasensitive level detection of Hg2+.

Infectious diseases caused by bacterial pathogens present a major worldwide healthcare challenge [34]. Among pathogenic bacteria strains, E. coli is one of the most abundant bacterial strains and one of the main contributors to global child mortality [35]. Therefore, methods for the rapid and sensitive detection of these bacteria are required along with cell inactivation. Currently, researchers have been developing nano-scale semiconductors for photo induced antibacterial applications [36]. Although some nanoparticles like TiO2 are in use as a semiconductor for antibacterial agents [37] but various organic molecules have to be used as probes for bio-imaging. However, they are not ideal agents for either bio-imaging or photoactive antibacterial treatment due to their lack of multiplexing, their instability, and their wide band gap of >3.0eV (UV range). Herein, we present the synthesis of highly fluorescent CDs using a low cost, solvent-free, eco-friendly method using mushrooms as a carbon precursor with many chemical constituents like vanillic, syringic and caffeic acid, 3,4-dihydroxy benzalacetone, polysaccharides, flavonoids, proteins and amino acids [38–40]. The synthesized mushroom CDs (MCDs) show excellent water dispersible, photochemical stability and fluorescence quantum yield (QY) (25%), which is higher than the recently reported method [41]. In addition, the synthesized MCDs have shown high Hg2+ detection sensitivity and have an ability to bind bacterial cell to act as a biocompatible cellular imaging tool with bactericidal activity.

Edible mushrooms (Pleurotus spp.) were obtained from a local market in Seongnam, South Korea and washed with Millipore water. Mercury nitrate monohydrate Hg(NO3)2·H2O and dihydrolipoicacid (DHLA) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (St. Louis, MO, USA). All remaining chemicals were purchased from local Korean chemical companies (Samchun Chemical and Daejung Chemical, Gyeonggi, Korea) at analytical grade and used without further purification. Millipore water was used to prepare all solutions in this study.

In contrast to common CD preparation methods, we did not use any solvent or additive chemical for the preparation of CDs from mushrooms as they have the sufficient (>90%) water [42], which can act as a solvent when evaporation was suppressed by a tightly sealed steel bomb reactor. Thus, we have developed a sustainable strategy for the synthesis of CDs by using edible mushroom as the sole source of carbon Fig. 1 . The MCDs were prepared by a one-step thermal treatment method. The mushrooms were cut to the appropriate volume and transferred to a Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave (50mL). The autoclave was heated at 170°C for 3h in an oven and then allowed to cool to room temperature. A dark brown liquid was obtained by filtration of the reaction mixture. The supernatant MCDs solution was separated from a centrifuge tube by centrifugation (10,000rpm, 20min) and dialyzed with a cellulose ester membrane (molecular weight cut-off: 3.5kDa) against DI water for 24h. The powder form of CDs was obtained by freeze-drying, and later CDs was dispersed in Millipore water to make final concentration of 1μgmL−1, the resulting MCDs were stored at 4°C in a refrigerator for further use. To optimize the reaction conditions, the synthesis procedure was performed at various temperatures (100, 140 and 170°C).

To achieve ultra-sensitivity and selectivity, the MCDs surfaces were functionalized with DHLA. In a 50mL round bottomed flask, 0.050g of DHLA and 0.005g of MCDs were mixed in 20mL of a 1:1 water and ethanol solution, followed by adjusting the solution pH to >9.5 by the addition of 0.10M NaOH with sonication for 10min. The reaction mixture was vigorously stirred for 2h at 60°C. The resulting solution was dialyzed with a cellulose ester membrane (molecular weight cut-off: 0.5–1kDa) overnight to remove unbound DHLA molecules. The resulting DHLA@MCDs were re-dispersed in DI water and stored at 4°C in a refrigerator for further use. The surface charges of the MCDs before and after functionalization at various pH values were analyzed by measuring the electro-kinetics (ζ potential) of the composites (ZetaPALS, Brookhaven Instrument Corp., USA). For the ζ potential measurement, the solution pH was adjusted from 2.0 to 12.0 by adding 0.1M HNO3 and 0.1M NaOH. The isoelectric point was strongly dependent on the surface functional groups.

Hg2+ detection was performed at room temperature. Solutions with various concentrations of Hg2+ metal ions (50μL) and 50μL of PBS (0.10M, pH 7.0) buffer solution were added to a cuvette containing 5.0μL of 0.120mg/L MCD solution with water added to form a total volume of 1000μL. The reaction mixture was incubated for 10min, and all photoluminescence (PL) spectra were recorded with excitation at 360nm. A similar methodology was applied for ultra-sensitive detection of Hg2+ metal ions using DHLA@MCDs. A similar experimental procedure was used to demonstrate Hg2+ ion detection selectivity.

For the bacterial imaging study, E. coli Nissle 1917 was used. This organism was grown at 37°C in Luria-Bertani (LB) agar media (Sigma Aldrich). A single colony of E. coli Nissle 1917 was inoculated in 5mL of LB nutrient broth and cultured overnight at 37°C. These freshly cultured bacteria were centrifuged at 5000rpm for 15min and washed twice with Millipore water. The bacterial pellet was re-suspended in 2mL of PBS (0.1M, pH 7.2) containing 10μL of MCD solution, which was further incubated for 1h at 37°C. Subsequently, the suspension was centrifuged to separate the MCD-labeled bacteria and the supernatant was removed; this process was repeated three times to remove the non-labeled MCDs. Finally, 10μL of MCD-labeled bacterial suspension was placed onto a microscope slide to obtain florescence images of bacterial cells under a Nikon Eclipse TE 2000-U laser scanning fluorescence microscope. Fluorescence images of the bacterial cells were recorded at 360, 470 and 520nm, which correspond to blue, green and red, respectively.

The bactericidal effect of MCDs by light irradiation toward E. coli Nissle 1917 was tested as per the following procedure. The freshly grown E. coli Nissle 1917 cells were centrifuged at 5000rpm for 15min, washed with Millipore water, and diluted with PBS solution at up to 106 CFU/mL concentrations. Then, 100μL of the MCD solution was mixed with 100μL of the bacterial suspension and placed on a LB agar plate. The plate was exposed to a visible LED light (2.7mW/cm2) for up to 12h. The same procedure was repeated under dark conditions, and bacteria without MCDs were also cultured and exposed to an LED light as a control experiment. All the experiments were repeated three times. Finally, MCD-treated plates with light-exposed, dark-treated and control experiment plates were incubated at 37°C overnight before the bacteria were counted to determine the viability number. To confirm MCDs influence on bacterial cell viability, we followed the standard MTT assay as described by Mossman et al., [43] with slight modifications. Typically, E. coli cells in a logarithmic growth phase are inoculated in a 96-well plate and cultured for 12h. Fresh culture medium containing MCD solution (treated in light and dark conditions) of various concentrations is added to cells. After further culturing for 20h, 20μL of MTT reagent was added to every well. The cells are cultured for another 12h in dark and light conditions. The culture medium is replaced by 150μL of DMSO. The absorbance of the mixture at 490nm is recorded with a Synergy H1 ELISA plate reader, and the cell viability was derived.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro reactive oxygen species (ROS) assay
ce:section.ce:para: ROS generation from MCDs was determined by using a commercially available ROS/RNS assay kit (OxiSelect™, Cell Biolabs Inc., San Diego, CA, USA). Three conditions (pristine MCDs, MCDs under dark and light irradiation conditions for 12h) were used for ROS assay by light irradiation. To quantify ROS, separate standard curves were prepared according to the procedures given with ROS assay kit. In a typical run, 50μL of MCD solution was treated to each condition batch with 50μL of catalyst solution in a well plate followed by incubation for 5min at room temperature. After 5min incubation, 100μL of 2′,7′-dichlorofluorescein diacetate (DCFH-DA) dye solution was added into each well and the plate was covered with aluminum foil to avoid the light followed by further incubation for 30–45min in dark condition. The fluorescence intensity is directly proportional to the amount of ROS presented in the population. The amount of ROS was calculated by calibration curve based on H2O2 standard solution in PBS. The fluorescence intensity was recorded at λex =480nm, λem =530nm in ambient conditions by using a fluorescence microplate reader (BioTek, FLx800 Fluorescence Reader, South Korea). The results are expressed as the mean±SD. Statistical analysis was performed with ANOVA analysis.

The phase purity of the MCDs was analyzed using a Rigaku Ultima III X-ray diffractometer equipped with a Cu sealed tube (λ=1.54178Å). The following conditions were used: 40kV, 30mA, increment=0.05°, scan speed=3°/min. Raman spectra were obtained using a LABRAM HR 800 micro-Raman spectrometer with an excitation laser wavelength of 515nm. Thermal gravimetric analysis (TGA) was carried out on a Linseis L81-I TG-DTA instrument at a heating rate of 10°Cmin−1 under N2 (flow rate: 20mLmin−1). The size and morphology of the biogenic MCDs were determined using transmission electron microscopy (TEM), and selected-area electron diffraction (SAED) patterns were obtained with an accelerating voltage of 300kV and camera constant of 28 mmÅ (Tecnai G2). A scanning electron microscope (SEM, S-4700, HITACHI, Japan) with an accelerating voltage of 20kV was used to determine the morphology of the bacterial cells with and without treatment with MCDs. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra of the MCDs and DHLA@MCDs before and after metal detection were recorded using a JASCO FTIR-4600 spectrometer in ATR mode equipped with a diamond window. Detailed structural and compositional studies were carried out using a PHI-Quantera II X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS) with a monochromatic Al Kα line X-ray setting (200μm, 50W, 15kV). UV–vis absorption spectra were recorded on a Shimadzu UV–vis spectrometer (UV-2550), and fluorescence emission spectra were obtained using a photoluminescence spectrometer (Quanta Master, Photon Technology International, NJ, USA) equipped with a xenon lamp (Arc Lamp Housing, A-1010B™), monochromator, and power supply (Brytexbox). Confocal laser scanning fluorescence microscopy images were recorded with a Nikon Eclipse TE 2000-U.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of MCDs and DHLA@MCDs
ce:section.ce:para: The size and morphology of the synthesized MCDs were characterized by TEM and high-resolution TEM (HRTEM). The CDs were uniformly dispersed and displayed a spherical shape with an average particle size of 4nm (Fig. 2 A, B), which is further illustrated by the particle size histogram with a mean diameter of 2–6nm (Fig. 2B inset). The HRTEM image in (Fig. 2C) shows that most of the particles were amorphous carbon nanodots (green circle) and that a few particles displayed lattice fringes (yellow circle) with lattice spacing of 0.218nm. An SAED image (Fig. 2D) of the MCDs does not show any diffraction spot or ring, indicating the amorphous nature of the MCDs. The amorphous and graphitic nature of the MCDs was further confirmed by XRD and Raman spectroscopy (Fig. S1A, B). The XRD pattern of the MCDs prepared at different temperatures (100, 140, and 170°C) are presented in (Fig. S1A, line i–iii). MCDs prepared at 100°C shows only one peak (2θ=16.9°), which is attributed to amorphous carbon (line i), whereas an additional peak appeared at 22.6°, corresponding to the presence of a partially disordered graphitic phase for MCDs prepared at 140 and 170°C (line ii and iii, respectively). In addition, the peak intensity at 22.6° for MCDs prepared at 170°C increased (line iii), which was attributed to the enhanced disordered graphitic phase [44]. The results reveal that increasing the synthesis temperature causes the increased formation of amorphous and graphitic carbon phases of the MCDs. Temperature-dependent uniform nucleation of bio-functional groups may lead to the formation of MCDs [45].

The nature of the carbon materials was further analyzed by the Raman spectroscopic study (Fig. S1 B). The Raman spectra of MCDs prepared at 170°C feature two peaks at 1370cm−1 (D band) and 1565cm−1 (G band) that are attributed to the disordered amorphous carbon and graphitic carbon atoms, respectively, indicating the presence of both sp3 carbon and sp2 hybridized carbon in the CDs [46]. In addition, the particle size can be calculated using Scherrer’s equation (D=0.89θ/βcosθ) and PXRD data, producing values of 2.8 and 3.9nm for 2θ at 16.9° and 22.6°, respectively. The calculated particle size is highly consistent with the size measured from the TEM image. The thermal stability of the MCDs (line i) and surface functionalized MCDs (line ii) was determined by TGA (Fig. S1C). Both materials show an initial weight loss at 137°C (4.1%) and 123°C (1.4%) for MCDs and DHLA@MCDs, respectively, attributed to the loss of water molecules. The second weight loss step between 150–330°C (45.8%) and 140–320°C (42.7%) for MCDs and DHLA@MCDs is due to the decomposition of organic moiety functional groups such as OH, CH, SH, CO and CO. Finally, the MCDs materials are totally decomposed to simple amorphous carbon at >350°C by the combustion of the aliphatic and aromatic domains of the carbon core. Moreover, the UV–vis absorption spectrum of DHLA@MCDs shows the absorbance of UV light at 290nm, which is assigned to the functionalization of DHLA on the surface of the MCDs (Fig. S1D, line i). The functionalization of DHLA was further confirmed by FT-IR and XPS analysis.

FT-IR spectra were used to identify the surface functional groups on the MCDs before and after functionalization with DHLA Fig. 3 . The IR spectra of the MCDs (line i) feature a broad band at 3346cm−1 corresponding to the stretching vibrations of both NH and OH. The peaks at 1070, 1325, 1633, 1712 and 2975cm−1 are related to CO, CN, CC, COOH and CH stretching vibrations respectively. This result indicates that the MCDs contain numerous amino, hydroxyl, and carboxylic functional groups [47], which results in the easy decoration of the MCD surface with other ligands. After functionalization with DHLA, the peaks at 3346, 1712, and 1325cm−1 were shifted to 3323, 1690, 1316cm−1, respectively, which indicates the amide bond formation via covalently between carboxylic group of DHLA and amine groups of MCDs [48]. In addition, new peaks appeared at 1682 and 2557cm−1 corresponding to the amide and presence of SH groups of DHLA@MCDS (Fig. 3, line ii). The FT-IR spectra clearly indicate that the DHLA was successfully decorated onto the MCDs surface and the DHLA decoration was further confirmed by XPS. The XPS survey spectrum of the MCDs reveals three peaks at 284.5, 400 and 530eV, which were attributed to C 1s, N 1s and O 1s, respectively. This finding indicates that MCDs mainly consist of carbon, nitrogen and oxygen (Fig. 4 A, line i). In addition to the peaks for carbon, however, nitrogen and oxygen, a new peak appeared at 164eV corresponds to S 2p of SH group in DHLA (Fig. 4A, line ii). The deconvolutions of high-resolution XPS data of C 1s, N 1s, O 1s and S 2p are shown in (Fig. 4B–E). The deconvolution of the C 1s spectrum (Fig. 4B, line i) of bare MCDs revealed the presence of four peaks corresponding to CC/CC (sp3, sp2), CN (amine), CO (alcohol) and COOH groups at 283.8, 285.1, 286.2, and 289.3eV. Additionally, the high-resolution O 1s spectrum (Fig. 4C, line i) revealed three peaks at 531.7, 532.5 and 533.2eV due to the presence of CO, COH/COC and COOH [49], respectively. In the case of nitrogen atoms (Fig. 4D, line i), two peaks at 399.5 and 400.9eV were assigned to the presence of NH and C3 N. It should be noted that the peaks of N 1s (NH) and O 1s (OH) are appeared at 399.8 and 532.9eV indicates functionalized of DHLA via amide and ester bond formations, which supports the FT-IR data. The high-resolution S 2p spectrum (Fig. 4E (ii)) was assigned peak at 163.9eV (S 2p3/2) indicating the presence of S-H groups. The above results confirm the formation of MCDs and successfully functionalization of DHLA onto the surface of MCDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The response of MCDs towards pH
ce:section.ce:para: The PL property of MCDs can be affected by various external parameters; thus, we studied the effect of pH. The PL intensity was recorded for MCD solutions with various pH values, namely, pH=2, 4, 5, 7.2, 9, 11, and 12 (Fig. S3A). The PL intensity of the MCD solution was quenched up to 35% as the pH value decreased, whereas only 9% quenching was observed at pH values higher than 7. This behavior may due to the protonation and deprotonation of the surface functional groups [53]. The effects of ζ potential on the MCDs before and after DHLA functionalization are shown in (Fig. S3B). After functionalization, the isoelectric point of bare MCDs was shifted to a lower pH value due to the presence of mercapto functional groups. In addition, the stability of the MCDs can be estimated based on the PL intensity change. The stability of the MCDs was investigated in the presence of salt solution and depending on storage duration. The PL of the MCDs was measured in the presence of various ionic concentrations of NaCl solution ranging from 0 to 500mM (Fig. S3C). No significant emission intensity change was observed, indicating that the MCDs are stable at high ionic concentrations. This result suggests that MCDs have great potential for sensing applications under physiological conditions [54]. In addition, the MCD stability after different storage time intervals was also investigated (Fig. S3D). MCDs showed 97% PL intensity for 120days. This percentage decreased sharply after 120days, to 72% at 180days. The high stability of the MCDs in various conditions allows applications of the material for bio-sensing and labelling. Moreover, to study the practical applicability of DHLA@MCDs sensor, we have investigated the effect of pH, NaCl, and concentration on the PL response to Hg2+ of DHLA@MCDs (Fig. S4 A–C).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensitivity and selectivity of MCDs towards Hg2+
ce:section.ce:para:
The fluorescence quenching of MCDs by the addition of Hg2+ ions allows application as a Hg2+ sensor. It may be because of the presence of nitrogen element on the MCDs surface, responsible for the Hg2+ sensing. The Hg2+ sensing the sensitivity of the MCDs toward various aqueous solution concentrations was analyzed, and unambiguous PL quenching by 47% was found upon the addition of Hg2+ ions to the bare MCDs (Fig. S5A). The fluorescence quenching shows the linearity from 0 to 100nM of Hg2+ (Fig. S5B), which corresponds to the detection limit of 4.13nM Hg2+ (see SI, equation S3). To achieve the extreme selectivity and sensitive of MCDs for the detection of Hg2+, a thiol based ligand, dihydrolipoic acid (DHLA), was further functionalized on the surface of the MCDs to produce DHLA@MCDs. The PL quenching of DHLA@MCDs in the presence of Hg2+ (Fig. 7 A) showed 93% quenching upon the addition of Hg2+ ions. The PL quenching ratio (F/F0) at 360nm can also be fitted to a linear function of metal ion concentration from 0 to 100nM with a high linear correlation coefficient (R2 =0. 999) for Hg2+ (Fig. 7B). The DHLA@MCDs showed a much lower detection limit (17.4 pM) for Hg2+ sensing than the bare MCDs (4.13nM). The attachment of thiol functional group on the surface of MCDs enhanced the Hg2+ detection sensitivity by more than two orders of magnitude, corresponding to one of the best values among the reported fluorescence Hg2+ sensing probes. The Hg2+ sensing properties of the other fluorescence probes are summarized in Table 1
[55–62]. The results indicate that the developed method shows one of the highest quantum yield, splendid sensitive, and selectivity for Hg2+ detection compared to the reported methods.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The quenching mechanism of MCDs and DHLA@MCDs for Hg2+
ce:section.ce:para: For the study of fluorescence quenching mechanism, we measured PL decay curve and calculated kinetic parameters before and after DHLA functionalization (Fig. S6, line i and ii). No significant kinetic parameter change after DHLA attachment to MCDs suggests similar charge separation and recombination is occurring in MCDs and DHLA@MCDs. After Hg2+ treatment, however, the average life time of DHLA@MCDs was significantly reduced from to 1.42±0.13ns (Fig. S6, line iii). The significantly decreased lifetime indicates the fluorescence was quenched by dynamic electron transfer from conduction band of MCDs to the LUMO energy level of Hg2+ ion [63]. Additionally, the UV–vis absorption spectrum of DHLA@MCDs shows that the absorption of UV light at 290nm was completely quenched after treatment with Hg2+ ions (Fig. S1D, line ii) indicating that Hg2+ ions can influence the surface functional groups of DHLA@MCDs. To study the Hg2+ binding site on the DHLA@MCDs, XPS and FT-IR spectra were analyzed after treatment with Hg2+ ions. A slight shifting of the SH band from 2557 to 2548cm−1 (Fig. 3, line iii) confirms that the Hg2+ ions are chelated with S-H functional groups. The survey XPS data of the DHLA@MCDs after metal treatment (Fig. 4A, line iii) shows a peak at 100.9eV, attributed to the binding energy of Hg 4f, whereas the binding energy of the S 2p is slightly shifted from 163.9eV to 164.3eV (Fig. 4E, line iii).

Due to the transfer of electrons from the SH groups to Hg, the S atoms become electron deficient, which results in the high PL quenching shown in (Fig. 7A) [64]. In the high-resolution XPS spectrum of mercury shown in (Fig. 4F), the peaks at 100.8eV and 105.2eV were Hg2+ energy levels 4f7/2 and 4f5/2, respectively. In addition, quantitative analysis of XPS data shows that the atomic ratio of Hg and S was 1:2.2, which demonstrates mercapto functional groups are act as an excellent chelating agent for Hg2+ ions (Table S1). Based on the photoluminescence, FT-IR and XPS analyses, we propose a Hg2+ binding and fluorescence mechanism that involves the chelation of the Hg2+ ion by two thiol functional groups on DHLA (Fig. 8 ). In summary, we can clearly found that the Hg2+ ions form a chemical bond to thiol functional groups of DHLA resulting in the fluorescence quenching due to fast electron transfer to LUMO of Hg2+ ion [65]. In case of other metal ions, however, weak binding to DHLA and unsuitable LUMO energy level of metal ions for fast electron transfer allow negligible fluorescence quenching (Fig. 7C) [66]. These results reveal that DHLA@MCDs has excellent selectivity for Hg2+ ion sensing. Because of the complexity of real environmental systems, a suitable fluorescent probe should exhibit both high sensitivity and selectivity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The selectivity of MCDs and DHLA@MCDs towards other metal ions
ce:section.ce:para: The selectivity and competition experiments were carried with using 100nM of diverse environmentally pertinent metal ions, including Mg2+, Fe3+, Co2+, Cu2+, Hg2+, Ni2+, Zn2+, Pb2+, Ag+, K+, Na+, Cd2+, Ca2+, Sr2+, Ba2+ and including amino acids (Fig. S7, S8). Fluorescence spectra were analyzed. The fluorescence intensity of the DHLA@MCDs was quenched to ∼92% in the presence of 100nM of Hg2+ ions, whereas other metal ions did not show significant quenching (Fig. 7C). In addition, the photographs of DHLA@MCDs in the presence of 100nM concentrations of different metal ions under bright and dark field UV illuminations are also presented in (Fig. 7D). This result clearly indicates that our sensing system is highly selective towards Hg2+ ions. Based on Pearson’s HSAB principle, a soft acid always tends to form bonds with a softer base. In this regard, S-H preferably binds with Hg2+ ions. Moreover, the selectivity behavior of the bare MCDs results are shown in (Fig. S7). In addition, detection of Hg2+ ions in the river and tap water samples with mixed ions were also performed (Fig. S9–S11, Table S2).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence imaging and photoinduced bactericidal study
ce:section.ce:para: For the imaging of bacteria, E. coli Nissle 1917 was labeled with bare MCDs by incubating at 37°C for 1h. A confocal micrograph of E. coli Nissle 1917 labeled with MCDs shows well-dispersed bacterial cells (Fig. S12). When the bacterial cells are excited at 360, 470 and 520nm, blue, green and red fluorescence was observed at the position of the cells, respectively (Fig. S12 B-D). This bright fluorescence emission from bacterial cells may indicate the internalization of MCDs into the cells via endocytosis. Due to their small size, unique surface functional groups and biocompatible nature, MCDs can be easily accumulated in the bacterial cell walls and cytoplasm of the cell. The results indicate that the MCDs are applicable as a novel probe for bio-imaging and medical diagnosis [67]. The strong light absorption property of MCDs may allow the generation of reactive oxygen species (ROS), which can be useful for killing cells [68,69]. The ROS generation of MCDs by light irradiation was studied using ROS/RNS assay kit. The wavelength at 400–500nm range has been found to generate oxyradicals, which response light induced bacterial cell death [70]. In our case, we have used 455nm visible light wavelength. The MCDs mediated ROS/RNS production was shown in (Fig. S13). The results show that, significant amount of ROS/RNS generation was occurred at 38.6±0.03, 51.5±0.09 and 88.4±0.16μM by the treatment of pure MCDs, MCDs treatment under dark and MCDs treatment with under visible light irradiation for 12h respectively. Most of the E. coli cells were damaged by the high ROS/RNS concentration (88.4±0.16μM) when it was treated with MCDs under visible light. Therefore, we studied the photoinduced bactericidal activity of the prepared MCDs.

To assess microbial cell viability, we employed a classic colony counting method. Bacterial colonies were incubated on a Petri dish with and without MCDs treatment and irradiated with visible light are shown in (Fig. S14). The presence of many bacterial colonies in the Petri dish LB agar plate not treated with MCDs (Fig. S14A) show no suppression of the bacteria. Similarly, relatively low bactericidal activity was observed for bacteria treated with MCDs without light irradiation. However, bacterial suppression can be clearly observed in bacterial cells treated with MCDs and irradiated with visible light (Fig. S14B). This result suggests that the bacterial cells are highly sensitive to visible light exposure when treated with MCDs. In addition, the bacterial cell viability was dependent on the light irradiation time, as summarized in (Fig. S14C, D). The sample treated with MCDs showed less than 10% bacterial cell viability upon irradiation with visible light for 12h. To corroborate the above colony counting method, we performed a microscopic investigation of the bacterial cells. The SEM images clearly show the E. coli Nissle 1917 cell morphology before and after the addition of MCDs. Bacterial cells with no MCDs in the presence of visible light show clear and smooth cell surfaces with a well-defined bacterial morphology (Fig. 9 A–C). When treated with MCDs without light irradiation, the bacterial surfaces are partially lesioned (Fig. 9D–F). Nonetheless, more than 80% of the bacterial cells are viable under dark conditions, which coincides with the cell viability result (Fig. S14D). In contrast, the bacterial cells treated with both MCDs and visible light are highly damaged (Fig. 9G–I). The cellular degradation was accompanied by the release of ROS, such as singlet oxygen from photo-excited MCDs, which cause cytoplasm and cellular disruption in the bacterial cells. Further the cytotoxicity of the MCD solution was confirmed via a standard MTT assay for E. coli bacterial cells. Fig. S15 illustrates that MCDs inhibit the growth of E. coli cells in the presence of light than the absence of light (dark). Of the tested concentrations, 80 and 100μL of MCDs shows significant inhibition of bacterial cell growth. Therefore, we can conclude the photo-excited CDs were effective in suppressing the growth of bacterial cells [71,72].

A plausible working mechanism for the bactericidal effect on E. coli cells is activity by free radicals under photodynamic therapy (PDT) shown in (Fig. S16). The SEM images clearly demonstrate that the E. coli bacterial cells are damaged by MCDs generating ROS under PDT, such as superoxide anion (O2 −.), and hydroxide radicals (HO.). Due to the semiconducting nature of the MCDs with a narrow band gap (1.49eV) between the valance band (VB) and conductance band (CB), photo-excited carriers are produced (ROS) from MCDs by irradiation with visible light, which can be used for the PDT (Fig. S16). The detailed proposed mechanism for the generation of ROS is shown in (see SI, equation S4-7). By light irradiation, holes in the VB react with water or hydroxyl ions and generate hydroxyl radicals. In addition, electrons in the CB react with oxygen dissolved in water and generate superoxide anion radicals. Those oxygen species are highly reactive, and they can directly attach to the surface of E. coli bacterial cells, leading to cell membrane destruction (Fig. 9G–I) [73]. This result clearly shows that the MCDs are useful in the imaging of bacterial cells and as a bactericidal material for photodynamic therapy.

Unlike many other CD synthetic procedures, herein, we present a new solvent-free synthesis of CDs from edible mushrooms. The MCDs show strong fluorescence with a high QY (25%). The prepared MCDs are spherical in shape and 4nm in size, and are composed of graphitic (sp2) and amorphous (sp3) carbon. Due to the presence of various functional groups containing oxygen and nitrogen, the MCDs are highly water soluble and easily functionalizable with various ligand molecules. The MCDs show high Hg2+ detection sensitivity (4.13nM) and the surface functionalization of MCDs with DHLA allows a substantial enhancement of sensitivity by more than two orders of magnitudes to produce one of the highest values (17.4 pM) among the reported Hg2+ sensors based on fluorescence. This probe also offers high sensitivity, even in real environmental samples (river and tap water) with a detection limit of 274 and 192 pM and more than 99% recovery. Due to the unique thiol functional group for Hg2+ binding, DHLA@MCDs feature high selectivity over other metal ions. Moreover, MCDs easily bind to E. coli, demonstrating biocompatible cellular imaging and bactericidal activity. In the presence of visible light, the growth of bacterial cells treated with the MCDs is totally inhibited. The reactive oxygen species generated by light irradiation destroy the bacterial cell membrane and enable the bactericidal activity of the MCDs. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first demonstration of biogenic CDs to show ultrasensitive detection of heavy metal ions, bio-imaging and photoinduced bactericidal activity. We believe our results allow for increased scalability in terms of green production and inspire research into the origin of chemical, biological and environmental functions of MCDs. Moreover, this material is also expected to be useful for solar cells, hydrogen production and in-vivo photodynamic applications.

M.Y., S.V. & A.S.R designed the study. S.V., A.S.R. & B.V. drafted the work, performed the research and M.Y. & S.V. wrote the article. All authors analyzed the results and approved the final version of the article.
The authors declare no competing financial interests.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by a National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) grant funded by the Ministry of Education (NRF-2014R1A1A2A16049980, NRF-2016M2A2A6024480, and NRF-2016R1D1A1B03930948). E. coli Nissle 1917 donated by C. S. Kathera, Sri Venkateswara University, Tirupati, India.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2017.11.044.

Further experimental details; XRD, FTIR, Raman, TGA, UV–vis absorption spectra, pH effect, Zeta potential measurement, Effect of NaCl, Storage time, Real water analysis, PL study, Lifetime decay and Photoinduced bactericidal activity along with Fig. S1–S16 and Table S1, S2 are available in Supplementary information.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Sada Venkateswarlu received his M.Sc and Ph.D., degrees in Inorganic Chemistry from Sri Venkateswara University, India in 2009 and 2014, respectively. During his Ph. D. course, he focused on the synthesis of magnetic nanomaterials and its applications. Later he joined as a postdoctoral fellow (2015–2017) under the supervision of Prof. Minyoung Yoon in Gachon University, Republic of Korea. He had expanded the research fields to the synthesis of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and metal-organic frames (MOFs) and their applications such as sensors, catalysis, energy storage and removal of toxic metals. He is currently working as an assistant professor in the Gachon University.

Buddolla Viswanath received his Master and Ph.D., Degrees in Microbiology from Sri Krishnadevaraya University, India. He is currently working as an assistant professor in the Department of Bionanotechnology, Gachon University, Republic of Korea. His research interests include applied Microbiology and Nanotechnology. He has published 32 research articles in Internationally reputed journals and authored 5 books.

Ankireddy Seshadri Reddy received his Ph. D. degree under the supervision of Prof. Jongsung Kim in 2017 and currently serving as an assistant professor in Gachon University, Republic of Korea. His research interests include nanomaterials and MOF-mediated biosensing, drug delivery, organic and inorganic synthesis, catalysis and renewable energy.

Minyoung Yoon received a B.S. degree and Ph.D. degrees in chemistry under the supervision of Prof. Kimoon Kim from POSTECH, Republic of Korea in 2005 and 2011, respectively. He worked in POSTECH and UC Berkeley as a postdoctoral research fellow from 2011 to 2013. He is currently working in Gachon University as an assistant professor from 2013. His research focuses on applications of novel porous materials and carbon based nano-materials for catalysis, gas separation, sensing, and transport.
